title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee approved several infrastructure projects and policy updates, including awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding reform, and establishing a $100/day fine for illegal sewer discharge violations. The committee also approved construction agreements for multiple road projects totaling over $200,000 in consulting and utility costs.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permits
Construction Services Proposals for Alderson/Jelnik and Bladel Projects

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally planned for 2029 but now likely moving to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project. Michael noted the village has secured over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating to fix road settlement over sewer trenches, and Schofield Avenue concrete repairs by HOS Construction.

Staff reported on spring road maintenance activities including removing road limits with Marathon County on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations on Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week, curb line sweeping, and hot mix patching. Jones Street experienced culvert failures requiring installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment, servicing mowers, and preparing vehicles for summer use. New Mac tandem axle chassis have been ordered.

Michael presented the annual report showing net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility value reached nearly $34 million, a 6.7% increase from infrastructure placed in service including wells 7 and 8 and PFAS settlement funds. The utility serves 6,100 customers total with 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in Schofield/Rib Mountain Metro. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael presented a League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supporting long-term transportation funding solutions. The resolution addresses the need for recurring annual funding for street maintenance and snow plowing rather than just one-time grant funding. Wisconsin was noted as generally affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership costs. Motion to adopt passed unanimously.

Michael explained the village's Ryan Street site stockpiles recycled concrete and asphalt that gets crushed into base course. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for an estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Michael demonstrated the value by comparing village costs of $17.11/cubic yard versus City of Wausau's $28/cubic yard, resulting in approximately $130,000 savings across three projects this year. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration into sanitary sewers, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. Discussion focused on residents illegally connecting sump pumps to sanitary sewers. Committee member Zagami suggested waiting for the inpipe technology INI study results before widespread enforcement. After discussion about distinguishing unknowing versus willful violations, the committee voted to direct staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented an agreement allowing a duplex at 2230 Radkkey Avenue to temporarily connect to water and sewer mains via long lateral service until Radkkey Avenue is reconstructed. Committee member Zagami requested adding a specific date, resulting in language specifying reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion passed unanimously with the date amendment.

The committee approved an intergovernmental agreement with Village of Rothschild for Military Road construction from Wolfman Street to Helin Street. Weston's share is $94,860 for utility costs ($6,156 sanitary sewer, $88,704 water main). Steam Construction is the contractor. The water main must be relocated due to storm sewer conflicts. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented late proposals for construction inspection services. For Alderson/Jelnik intersection project, MSA bid $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910. For Bladel Avenue, MSA bid $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730. Michael recommended MSA for the roundabout project due to their involvement in design, but preferred Clark Dietz for Bladel due to having full-time experienced staff rather than a summer intern. Motion to approve MSA for roundabout and Clark Dietz for Bladel passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton (estimated $126,900)
Village Board to adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding reform
Staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fine
Approve temporary water/sewer connection agreement for 2230 Radkkey Avenue with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild for $94,860 in utility costs
Award construction services: MSA for Alderson/Jelnik roundabout ($37,315), Clark Dietz for Bladel Avenue ($73,730)
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for Thursday, May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Staff to provide cost analysis of clear water infiltration treatment costs for next meeting
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday this week

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request: Taste and Glow Balloonfest

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated and elected as Chair unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair, seconded by Jackson, and elected unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as Secretary unanimously. There was brief procedural confusion about whether the Vice Chair nomination had been seconded.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) presented on behalf of the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. Commission members questioned room night tracking, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a request that organizers provide better room tracking data from hotels in their post-event report. Motion passed unanimously.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, noting the candidates toured Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese. She mentioned this was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County since the 1970s. The winner, Anastasia, sat at their table and was from South Dakota State with three bachelor's degrees.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest for their annual event
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room tracking data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved the site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with the commission taking steps to protect the village's drinking water supply.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility site plan
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Chief Jeremy explained that the building was rotated to face east due to a gas pipeline running under the parking lot, which significantly reduced fill costs. The building will accommodate 50-60 officers for future growth, up from current staffing of 40 officers. Commissioner Zagami made a motion to recommend approval subject to staff conditions 2-5. Discussion included questions about landscaping compliance, gas pipeline arrangements with ANR, and future expansion possibilities to the north. The motion passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Jennifer presented the new inspection program requiring two annual inspections per salt storage facility in wellhead protection areas - one in warm weather focusing on structural integrity and one in winter focusing on operations. A standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards will be used. Staff noted one existing facility near the village well on Sternberg that never went through the wellhead protection conditional use process. Commissioner Lumper moved to approve the program with staff reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee. The motion passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented. Passed unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: April 2026 staff approved items and building permits
discussions.body: Both the certified survey maps/site plans and building permits for April 2026 were approved unanimously without discussion.

discussions.item: Planning department monthly update and future items
discussions.body: Jennifer announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan will be Monday, June 1st at 5:00 PM. Discussion also touched on potential residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with Jennifer noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with the commission or incorporated into comprehensive plan discussions.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke remotely. He requested the commission exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, urging staff to waive excessive requirements similar to what was done for the Greenheck Turner building. He also asked that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on site visits and findings, noting there are not many such facilities in wellhead protection zones.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive recommended for approval to Board of Trustees
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted with two annual inspections per facility
Staff to report inspection findings to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee next year
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that lacks conditional use approval
Joint Plan Commission and Village Board steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 PM
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8, 2026 at 6:00 PM

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an introductory meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from department heads on the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Service Office, and Aging Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and heard detailed overviews of each department's mission, services, staffing, and budget structure.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call (noting remote participation)
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute - overview of agenda structure
Review of standing committee ordinance section 2.04, missions, duties, and responsibilities
Health Department presentation by Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka
Social Services Department overview by Administrator Leonard
Veteran Service Office presentation by VSO Erin Galindo
Aging Disability Resource Center presentation by Executive Director Mike Ray

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction under section 2.04 sub 5, covering departments (Veteran Service, Social Services, Health Department) and affiliated entities (North Central Health Care, ADRC of Central Wisconsin). He outlined reporting relationships with statutory bodies including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, North Central Healthcare Board, and Board of Health.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting against environmental hazards. The department has 37.6 FTE staff across four divisions: environmental health (1,200+ licenses), nursing (communicable disease and family health), community health improvement, and administration. She highlighted the new homeless system response position, public health vending machines with free resources including tick kits, and the Nurse Family Partnership program with its first graduates. About half of the $5 million budget is tax levy, with environmental health largely self-supporting through fees.

Administrator Leonard presented for absent Director Christa, explaining the department's 140 FTE staff handle child welfare, youth justice, children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. He highlighted the successful Family Keys pilot program addressing housing instability, noting many participants had never had a bank account or formal lease. The department's $22 million budget includes about $7 million in tax levy, with some programs 100% reimbursed and others fully tax levy supported.

VSO Erin Galindo reported his small but mighty 3.0 FTE office had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up from 5,900 the previous year. He emphasized surviving spouse benefits through dependency and indemnity compensation ($1,800-$2,400/month tax-free), housing stability partnerships with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention efforts (7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans). The office generated $44 million annually in benefits for veterans and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Galindo noted the staff shortage and 6-week booking backlog when understaffed, now reduced to under 3 weeks.

Executive Director Mike Ray explained the four-county (Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, Wood) organization provides aging and disability resources, Meals on Wheels, Medicare counseling, falls prevention, and dementia care support with about 60 FTE and 385-400 volunteers. He noted significant financial challenges with nutrition costs up 40-45% over four years while federal Older Americans Act funding remained flat. The organization hadn't requested a levy increase from 2011 until 2024, finally receiving one in 2025. Marathon County's contribution is $413,135.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation
Strategic plan discussion to be added to future agenda by Chair Boots
Next meeting confirmed for Wednesday, June 3rd at 3:00 PM (corrected from initially stated May 29th)
Department heads to provide additional presentations as requested by committee members

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinances including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and made committee appointments.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
Acknowledge reports from committees and commissions with chair updates
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bikes
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases from $300 per single family unit to $761, $250 to $563 for duplex units, and $250 to $495 for multifamily units, based on the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the justification for the numbers without detailed park maintenance cost data. Trustee Motti supported the fee as justified given population growth. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

The board debated amendments to section 1.08 of the elected and appointed village officials handbook. Discussion centered on removing language requiring members to justify remote attendance based on health, safety, or welfare concerns. Trustees Beck and Clark motioned to strike sentences requiring specific justifications, keeping only the requirement to notify 12 hours in advance, be on camera, and exclude participation in closed sessions. The amendment passed with two no votes from Hermling and Motti. The original motion as amended also passed with two no votes from the same trustees.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, passed unanimously. Parks, Finance/HR, and CLIPS still have openings. Board agreed to include committee applicant resumes in future meeting packets.

Chief Finkey explained the ordinance creates reciprocal fire inspection agreements between Weston, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, Cloverleaf, and Riverside. Once a food truck is inspected by one participating department, a sticker indicates compliance for all communities, eliminating redundant inspections. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Secondhand dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC
discussions.body: Chief Hunt explained the license requirement under state statute 134.71 for businesses buying and selling precious metals. The board amended the motion to require completion of background checks and receipt of occupancy permit since the business location at 5703 Jamar Street is still under construction. Approved unanimously as amended.

Board held discussion-only session on the farmers market relocation from the former Camp Phillips/Scoffield location to Kennedy Park after the property owner decided to develop the original site. Concerns raised about parking on grass at Kennedy Park creating safety issues and potential turf damage. Trustee Beck expressed concern about cars parked on grass along Alderson. Renee explained the market grew from 25 to 53 vendor spots and vendors prefer having vehicles with them. Mock Miller Park was discussed but rejected due to distance concerns that hurt attendance at the previous municipal building location. No action taken; monitoring will continue.

Public Works Director Michael Dolan reported on the ongoing speed limit study for Weston Avenue. DOT engineering study indicated 35 mph but the 85th percentile speed measured was 45 mph. A second study is needed but was delayed due to construction. A resident noted the speed is currently inconsistent with the ordinance two months after the board directed a change. Item will return to the board, possibly at a joint meeting.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of Deon 52 Street spoke during public comment criticizing the hiring of outside engineering consultants for $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled. He also criticized the appointment process for committees, noting Lisa Beck was introduced at a SAFER meeting before official board appointment. He requested detailed information on Kennedy Park funding be compiled in one document and called for greater administrator accountability. During the impact fee hearing, Pitel again spoke questioning the lack of specific park maintenance cost data to justify the fee amounts.
actionItems: Ordinance 26-010 increasing park and recreation impact fees approved
Resolution 2026-015 amending village fee schedule approved
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Mobile food vendor license ordinance 26-008 approved
E-bike ordinance 26-012 approved
Nuisances ordinance 26-011 approved
Standing committees ordinance 26-009 approved
Resolution 2026-014 supporting transportation fund solution approved
Secondhand dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC approved pending background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Intergovernmental service agreement with Ringle for building inspections to be developed
Comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6 PM
Speed limit issue for Weston Avenue to return to board
Committee applicant resumes to be included in future meeting packets

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to at-large executive committee positions, and unanimously passed a resolution to reduce landfill tipping fees for storm debris following a tornado that devastated the Ringle area. The board suspended its rules to address the emergency tipping fee matter directly without committee review.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments presentation by Chair Gibbs
Election of at-large executive committee members
Ballot counting for executive committee election
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, noting goals including increasing representation, allowing supervisors to develop as leaders, and accommodating preferences. He noted 34% of the board is in their first or second term. The appointments were approved but not unanimously on a single motion that included both county board chair and administrator appointments.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained she nominated Leur because she had seen her advocate for the county's smallest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were distributed and supervisors selected two names. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. No discussion occurred. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. No discussion occurred. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Resolution 26-26: Landfill Tipping Fees Modification for Storm Debris
discussions.body: Administrator Leonard explained the Solid Waste Management Board held a special meeting to address debris from severe weather including a tornado that devastated Ringle. The state waived $13.10 per ton, Ringle waived $2.33, and the county proposed reducing fees to $36.94 per ton (commercial rate minus waivers) to cover costs. Director Hagen Busher noted 400 tons of right-of-way debris was already received. The board suspended rules unanimously to consider the resolution without committee review. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were allowed to speak and requested the county fully waive fees. Multiple supervisors discussed tracking costs, insurance reimbursement issues, and potential future policy. The resolution passed unanimously.

publicComment: Two residents were granted special permission to speak on the tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty asked the county to fully wave fees for six months, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle were waving their fees and that businesses had donated over $40,000 in cleanup efforts. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of homeowners awaiting demolition, noting 44 homes affected and many still waiting for insurance adjusters, requesting more time and relief for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approved for out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees to approximately $36.94 per ton for storm debris through June 17, 2025
Administrator to continue discussions with Community Foundation regarding potential reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop a clear policy on handling similar disaster situations within six months
Staff to track all storm-related debris with certification forms for potential FEMA reimbursement

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified tornado relief fee waiver proposal to the county board. The committee amended the fee waiver to only cover septic system reconnect fees ($160) for properties damaged by the April 17, 2026 tornado, rather than the broader waiver originally proposed by staff.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (urban residential/general agriculture to rural residential) - Town of Green Valley
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture) - Town of McMillan
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Department overview: Solid Waste Department presentation by Dave Hagenbusher
Department overview: Parks, Recreation and Forestry presentation by Tom Lovelin
Department overview: Conservation Planning and Zoning presentation by Lori Miskimmons

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made the motion to approve and Supervisor Drebeck seconded. The motion carried with all in favor.

discussions.item: Rezoning: Tim Veland on behalf of James Offer and David Tract
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential in the Town of Green Valley. The Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact, conclusions of law, and town approval. Supervisor Kurt seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

Garrett Pogle presented the petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in the Town of McMillan. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy nature and potential environmental impact. Pogle explained the property currently has a home and horse riding stable, and rezoning would make the existing uses conforming rather than non-conforming. He confirmed no natural areas would be lost. The Town Board recommended approval April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve; Supervisor Drew seconded. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather damage
discussions.body: Director Lori Miskimmons presented staff recommendations for waiving county zoning and septic fees for tornado-damaged properties. Administrator Lance Leonhard noted this follows the governor's emergency declaration. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about fairness compared to isolated incidents. Supervisor Leur asked about tracking costs. After discussion, Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve the full proposal; Supervisor Leur seconded. Supervisor Drebeck then offered an amendment to limit the waiver to only pouts reconnect fees ($160). Supervisor Kurt seconded the amendment. The amendment passed with opposition. The amended motion to forward to county board passed but was not unanimous.

actionItems: Forward rezoning petition for James Offer and David Tract (Green Valley) to County Board for approval
Forward rezoning petition for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz (McMillan) to County Board for approval
Forward amended fee waiver resolution to County Board - waiver limited to pouts reconnect fees only ($160) for properties damaged by April 17, 2026 tornado
Track costs and requests related to tornado fee waivers for future policy development
County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin retiring June 1st; Joe Tucker selected as replacement

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center to next month due to the chair's absence, approved kayak launch signage for three village locations, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and trail maintenance issues. The meeting included public comment opposing the dog day concept and criticism of Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: A motion was made and seconded to approve the previous meeting minutes. The motion carried with no opposition.

Director Sean Oster provided a timeline of the Kennedy Park project from the 2023 comprehensive plan approval through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Scott questioned fundraising timelines, and staff clarified the village's agreement allows approximately 10 years for project completion. The Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for field lights, having raised approximately $84-85,000 toward a $117,000 goal. Staff noted the friends group plans a public check presentation at their June tournament. Committee members emphasized the Friends group deserves grace as volunteers and that master plans are guidance documents subject to change.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan provided photos and a PDF showing the approximate location of trees removed from the river, including images of wood mats installed to protect gas pipelines during equipment access. The mats, which are 8x8 timbers, are now village property with extras available for other trail projects. TC Energy (formerly ANR pipeline) representatives were on-site during the work.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: The committee deferred this item to the June meeting because Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. One audience member who spoke during public comment expressed opposition to the concept, stating animals should not be allowed in public pools.

Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches, noting pressure washing equipment cannot access the trail due to bridge width limitations and terrain. Discussion included applying anti-vandalism coatings or sealers and potentially organizing volunteer workdays. Staff will investigate cleaning options and report back. No formal action was taken.

Dan Verheyen from 2625 Northwestern Avenue presented version seven of signs for the Plover River water trail. Signs will be installed at three village locations: Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks. The signs include a map, QR code for river gauge, and location-specific history. Marathon County Parks Foundation is funding the signs through state waterway grants. Motion to approve installation passed unanimously.

Staff mentioned receiving a request to install food allergy awareness signs on playgrounds, which would be provided at no cost. Committee members agreed to bring this back for future discussion and education, acknowledging concerns about setting precedent for other signage requests.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. Andy from 6504 [address unclear] spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' Jim Pensan (appearing online under the name 'Vern M') from 5002 [street] Weston criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation, questioned Kennedy Park financial reporting calling the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental,' demanded to know where grant money and Glow event proceeds went, and accused the Friends of Kennedy Park of being 'frauds' for not sharing detailed finances.
actionItems: Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June 22nd meeting
Staff to investigate cleaning solutions and anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail amenities
Village to install kayak launch signs at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks locations - approved unanimously
Food allergy awareness playground signage to be brought back for future discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11th, 5-7 p.m. in training room

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged budget reports before receiving a detailed educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam about the clerk's office operations, including elections, licensing, tax collection, and staffing. Committee member Evan announced he will be stepping down as this was his last meeting.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget and office overview
Staff remarks and farmers market discussion

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve was made by Ernling and seconded by Daniels. The motion carried with no discussion.

discussions.item: TIFF one and TIFF two detail reports
discussions.body: A committee member asked about the final numbers for TIF closure. Staff explained they are anticipating October/November for bond payoff, stating the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds. Staff confirmed they will be able to pay off the debt and explained the process involving Ellers and US Bank Trust.

discussions.item: Acknowledgment of work product transmittals (items 5, 6, and 7)
discussions.body: Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIFF detail reports, and legal details report was made by Daniels and seconded by Soaper. Motion carried unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented comprehensive overview of the clerk's office operations. She noted the office went from four full-time staff to current staffing of two full-time and one part-time employee. She detailed election administration including Badger Book electronic poll books ($11,000 for four units), explained that 84% of tax collection ($22 million) is done at the clerk's office counter, and discussed pet licensing which was brought back in-house from a third-party vendor. Discussion occurred about the pet license fee increase from $10 to $20, which was driven by Marathon County raising fees to cover their $100,000 annual payment to the Humane Society. The quarterly newsletter costs approximately $30,000 annually for postage and printing to 5,089 postal patrons.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well at Kennedy Park, with full vendor capacity including food trucks, coffee vendors, and craft people. Produce vendors with greenhouses are present and more will come mid to end of June. A committee member raised concerns about parking on Alderson Street, with staff clarifying only the west side allows parking now. Discussion touched on parking congestion during baseball tournaments and other events.

Committee member Evan announced he will be stepping down as this was his last meeting, noting he and his wife are moving away from Weston but staying in the metro area. The chair thanked him for his service and noted the committee now has open spots needing to be filled.

actionItems: Minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting approved
April budget status report, TIFF detail reports, and legal details report acknowledged
CDA resolution needed for bond payoff expected this summer
Committee seeking citizen volunteers to fill open committee spots
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee approved amendments to Chapter 50 nuisances, recommended an e-bike ordinance with added emoto restrictions, and authorized the razing of an abandoned structure at 4104 Shore Avenue. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations and a crosswalk request, deferring decisions on both items pending additional information.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Department reports - Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police
Mobile food vendors fire inspection amendment

discussions.body: Code Enforcement reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement roll after the property was sold at auction and the new owner submitted repair permits. Chief Hunt reported the police department had 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with the 38th starting last week, 39th in May, and 40th around January. Two SROs work with zoning during summer months. Motion by Sherwanka to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee finalized amendments including switching tree limb height requirements to 14 and 10 feet over streets and walkways, with added language requiring compliance with the jurisdiction having authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campey, to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees passed unanimously.

Extended discussion focused on whether to allow emotos (electric dirt bike-style vehicles) in the village. Chief Hunt strongly recommended prohibiting emotos on roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee discussed electric scooters but decided to leave discretion to officers. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, to recommend approval with the addition that emotos are prohibited from operating on roadways, sidewalks, trails, or any public property in the Village of Weston passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4 AM to 4 PM) is significantly longer than surrounding municipalities which typically have 4-5 hour windows. Public Works Director Michael noted plowing doesn't typically reach residential streets until 7-8 AM. Chief Hunt opposed eliminating overnight parking entirely, stating it gives officers time to clear vehicles before plowing begins. Committee discussed uniformity with other municipalities and decided to defer the item until July, with Michael to identify specific problem areas that need signage.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between the church and DC Everest High School parking lot. Staff estimated cost at approximately $2,000 for a basic crosswalk or $25,000-$30,000 for a lighted system. Committee expressed concern about setting precedent for paying for crosswalks. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Sherwanka, that if Mount Olive is willing to cover the cost of installing the crosswalk, the committee recommends board approval. Motion passed unanimously. Public Works Director will contact Mount Olive.

Fire Chief explained a regional partnership with Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Safer to conduct mutual food truck fire inspections. One inspection covers all participating municipalities for the season. Fire Inspector Marty recently failed one truck that had improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Motion by Sherwanka, seconded by Campey, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: 5915 Delicoski Street (unknown duplex needing second address), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence with an undiscovered apartment unit, and 2211 Radkey Avenue needing correction for a new duplex on a private drive. Chief Hunt also identified Weston Villa Apartments on Delicoski as having separate addresses for each of eight units plus a utility room. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval to the Board of Trustees.

Staff requested authorization to begin raze proceedings on an abandoned structure that has been posted since 2021 and abandoned since approximately 2010-2014. A previous fire training burn was cancelled because the structure was too deteriorated for safe training. Chickens were discovered housed inside during a previous investigation. Current owners are up to date on taxes and prefer the village proceed with razing, with costs added to the tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location, predicting the park's grassy areas would be destroyed by vehicles parking on grass, and noted current village code prohibits parking on grass. He questioned whether ADA-accessible parking spaces on grass meet requirements for firm, level surfaces. He asked where the fire inspection requirement for mobile food vendors originated. He raised concerns about food truck regulations on public property and suggested code violation cases should receive regular progress updates, noting some cases have been pending for years with no visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisances amendments to Board of Trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition to Board of Trustees for approval
Public Works Director to identify specific areas needing overnight parking signage and bring back to July meeting
Public Works Director to contact Mount Olive Church about paying for crosswalk installation
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to Board of Trustees for approval
Authorize staff to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster outside
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff

The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers

The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
